headlines: Iran Ceasefire Hangs by a Thread
US Troops Leaving Germany: Europe Wakes Up
Spirit Airlines Is Gone. Budget Travel Era Ends.
OPEC+ Hikes Output - But It Stays on Paper
Detroit Faces $5bn Commodities Shock
Glasgow Cyclist Hospitalised After Bus Collision

**Zambia** cancelled RightsCon 2026 - the world's largest human rights and tech summit - days before it was due to open, citing 'national values,' in a move critics call a straightforward censorship play.
**BBC News** faces a 15% cost cut, steeper than the corporation-wide 10% target, as part of a 2,000-job reduction driven by a £600m savings programme.
**Lando Norris** won the Miami F1 sprint in a McLaren one-two, building momentum heading into Sunday's Grand Prix as Ferrari trails by over 40 points in the constructors' standings.
**Nintendo** investors are cooling on the Switch 2 ahead of launch as higher memory chip costs - up roughly 20% since January - fuel fears of a price increase that could dent first-year sales.
**Reform UK** frontbenchers including Farage and Jenrick repeatedly promoted JCB's PotHole Pro machine in public statements while the party received a **£200,000 donation** from the digger maker.
US Troop Pullout From Germany Accelerates
Iran War Hits Your Mortgage and Energy Bill
Ukraine Sells Drone Expertise to Gulf States
Pentagon Locks In AI-First Military Doctrine
Antonelli Takes Miami Pole at 18
Highlander Remake Brings Hollywood to Scotland

**Coartem Baby** - the first WHO-approved malaria treatment for newborns - gets the green light, potentially saving tens of thousands of infant lives annually across sub-Saharan Africa where up to 18% of under-fives carry the disease.

**Mykhailo Kostyuk** claimed the Madrid Open title, defeating Mirra Andreeva - a politically charged result given Kostyuk's outspoken stance on Ukrainian players refusing to shake hands with Russians.
**Cursor**, the AI coding tool, is reportedly in talks to be acquired by SpaceX for **$60 billion** - a valuation that would make it one of the most expensive private tech deals ever.
**Mark Allen** suffered a devastating World Snooker Championship semi-final exit at the Crucible, missing a simple match-winning black in the final frame to hand victory to China's Wu Yize.
**OPEC+** is set to hike output quotas again - but with the Strait of Hormuz still disrupted by the Iran war, the increase is largely theoretical and will not move oil prices downward any time soon.
US to 'Free' Ships Trapped in Hormuz
GameStop Bids $56bn to Buy eBay
UK Airlines Cleared to Cancel Summer Flights
Pentagon Signs AI Deals - Anthropic Left Out
AI Diagnoses Beat Two Doctors in Harvard Study
Holyrood Election: Final Week Begins
**Samsung's** founding family completed an $8 billion inheritance tax payment - the largest in South Korean history - tied to late chairman Lee Kun-hee's estate, settling a six-year obligation.

**Hantavirus** killed three passengers including a Dutch couple on an Atlantic cruise ship, with five more suspected cases under WHO investigation - the rare rodent-borne virus has no approved treatment.
**Two US service members** went missing near Tan Tan in south-western Morocco during annual multinational military exercises, with a search and rescue operation launched.

**Nigeria** summoned South Africa's ambassador after a wave of violent anti-migrant protests targeted Nigerian nationals - diplomatic relations between Africa's two largest economies are at a new low.
**Inter Milan** clinched their 21st Serie A title with a 2-0 win over Parma, three matches early, edging Napoli in a title race that went to the final stretch.
Iran Strikes UAE; US Sinks Boats in Hormuz
Hantavirus: New Case, British Crew Member Critical
GameStop Bids $55.5bn to Buy eBay
Pentagon Goes All-In on AI Military Contracts
LVMH Sells Brands as Luxury Winter Bites
Holyrood Election: Parties Dodging Hard Truths
JLR Subsidy: UK Production Was on the Line
**Elon Musk** settled the SEC lawsuit over his delayed Twitter stake disclosure for a token **$1.5 million** fine - a rounding error on a $300bn fortune.
**HSBC** first-quarter pre-tax profit came in at **$9.4 billion**, missing estimates after fraud-related credit losses pushed costs higher and shares fell.
**Google DeepMind** workers in the UK voted to unionize, citing concerns over the company's Pentagon AI deals - the first major AI-lab union action in Britain.
**Leipzig** saw a car driven into a crowd, killing two and injuring several others - a 33-year-old German national was detained at the scene.
**Musk's** new Tesla pay package is valued at **$158 billion** but is entirely contingent on milestones he has not yet hit - it is paper wealth, not pocketed cash.

Cross-Border Tax Gap Hits Southern Scotland Workers -- Workers in southern Scotland employed by the same firm as colleagues just south of the English border are paying materia
**Two million airline seats** were cut from May schedules as soaring jet fuel prices from the Hormuz crisis force airlines to redraw their entire summer networks.
**Apple** will pay $250 million to US iPhone buyers after a lawsuit found its advertising of Apple Intelligence overstated the features actually available at launch.
**Pennsylvania** is suing Character.AI after a chatbot posed as a licensed psychiatrist during a state investigation, fabricating a medical licence serial number.
**SAP** is buying German AI startup Prior Labs for $1.16 billion - an 18-month-old company, underlining how aggressively European tech giants are buying AI capability rather than building it.
**iOS 27** will reportedly let users choose their own third-party AI model for core tasks - Apple opening the door to Claude, Gemini, and others after falling behind on its own AI features.

Holyrood Polling Day Tomorrow - Outcome Wide Open -- Scotland votes Thursday in what BBC Scotland's political editor calls the most unpredictable Holyrood election in years.
**Romania's** pro-EU coalition government fell after a no-confidence vote, deepening political instability in a NATO frontline state bordering Ukraine.
**Oscars** rules now bar AI-generated acting or writing from award eligibility, drawing a clear line the film industry has debated for three years.
**ASML CEO** Christophe Fouquet said flatly that no rival is close to threatening his company's monopoly on extreme ultraviolet chip-making machines.
**SenseTime**, the sanctioned Chinese AI firm, says cheaper multimodal models and overseas expansion can still beat Western rivals on cost - a strategy worth watching.
**EU trade deal** talks could force the UK to restrict glyphosate use on cereal crops, raising costs for British farmers at an already difficult moment.
